title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Indonesia — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Kopi Tubruk
items.text: Ground coffee grounds settle at the bottom of your cup—wait a moment before sipping so you don't get a mouthful of sediment. It's strong, unfiltered, and often served with sweetened condensed milk (kopi susu) or black (kopi hitam).

items.label: Jamu and Coffee Blends
items.text: Many Indonesian coffee shops blend their kopi with turmeric, ginger, or other traditional herbal remedies (jamu). Ask what's in it before ordering if you have preferences or allergies.

items.label: Warung Kopi vs. Café
items.text: Cheap street-level warungs serve authentic kopi tubruk for under 10,000 IDR; trendy cafés in Jakarta and Bali charge 40,000+ IDR for specialty roasts. Both are worth visiting for different reasons.

items.label: Sugar is Standard
items.text: Indonesian coffee is traditionally very sweet. If you want it less sweet or black, say 'sedikit gula' (little sugar) or 'tanpa gula' (no sugar) when ordering.

items.label: Rendang
items.text: Meat (usually beef) slow-cooked in coconut milk and spice paste until rich and concentrated. This is Indonesia's most famous dish—try it in Padang restaurants for authentic versions, not watered-down tourist versions.

items.label: Soto Ayam
items.text: Turmeric-based chicken soup with rice, typically served with a hard-boiled egg and potato. Order it for breakfast or lunch; it's warming, affordable, and available everywhere from street stalls to restaurants.

items.label: Gado-Gado
items.text: Blanched vegetables (cabbage, bean sprouts, green beans) topped with peanut sauce, fried tofu, and a hard-boiled egg. It's vegetarian-friendly and a reliable lunch option; sauce quality varies wildly so eat at busy stalls with high turnover.

items.label: Nasi Goreng
items.text: Fried rice cooked with soy sauce, garlic, chili, and usually egg and protein. Every cook makes it differently; it's simple comfort food but tourist restaurants often charge 5× the price of warung versions.

items.label: Bakso
items.text: Beef meatball soup served in a savory broth with noodles, vegetables, and fried shallots. It's cheap, hearty, and found at dedicated bakso stalls; ask for 'bakso daging' (beef) to avoid organ-meat versions.

items.label: Satay [Sate]
items.text: Skewered meat grilled and served with peanut sauce (often with a squeeze of lime and sliced shallots). Chicken, beef, and goat are common; the meat quality and sauce make all the difference—stick to busy stalls.

items.label: Point and Ask
items.text: Many warungs have no English menus—point at what other diners are eating or ask 'Apa yang bagus?' (What's good?). Locals are usually happy to recommend the day's best dish.

items.label: Spice Levels
items.text: Indonesian food is often spicy; say 'tidak pedas' (not spicy) or 'sedikit pedas' (a little spicy) when ordering. They will respect this—don't be shy about asking.

items.label: Restaurant vs. Warung Pricing
items.text: Restaurants with printed menus and table service will charge 2–5× more than street-level warungs for similar food. Neither is 'better,' but understand the price difference reflects service and ambiance, not quality.

items.label: Rice is a Staple, Not Automatic
items.text: Many dishes come with rice included, but confirm by asking 'dengan nasi?' (with rice?). Some noodle dishes already include carbs, so you may not want extra.

items.label: Avoid Uncooked Vegetables in Street Food
items.text: Raw vegetables in warung salads and fresh spring rolls can carry water-borne parasites if not handled properly. Order cooked options or eat at busier, cleaner-looking stalls with visible food preparation.

items.label: Warung Kopi
items.text: Humble coffee shops open early (5–6am) serving kopi, eggs, toast, and simple noodles. These are where locals eat breakfast and are cheap, authentic, and reliable for genuine Indonesian food.

items.label: Pasar Malam (Night Markets)
items.text: Temporary markets set up in the evening selling street food from dozens of stalls. Prices are rock-bottom, variety is high, and they're socially lively—go with a local or arrive early while stalls are fresh.

items.label: Rumah Makan (Family Restaurants)
items.text: Mid-range sit-down restaurants serving regional Indonesian cuisine. They're slightly pricier than warungs but offer consistent quality, English menus, and a good introduction to regional dishes.

items.label: Food Courts in Malls
items.text: Modern shopping centers have food courts with multiple Indonesian and Asian vendors, clean facilities, and prices between warungs and restaurants. Useful when you want choice and hygiene certainty.

items.label: Tourist Restaurant Traps
items.text: Restaurants in Seminyak (Bali), central Jakarta, and other tourist areas drastically inflate prices and reduce authenticity. Eat where locals eat—one block away from main tourist streets, prices often drop 50%.

items.label: Teh Tarik (Pulled Tea)
items.text: Strong black tea mixed with condensed milk, traditionally poured from one cup to another to create a frothy top. It's sweet, creamy, and iconic—order at any warung kopi or Malaysian-Indonesian café.

items.label: Jus Buah (Fresh Fruit Juice)
items.text: Pressed juice from local fruits (mangoes, papayas, watermelon, passion fruit) blended with ice and sometimes condensed milk. Cheap and refreshing; order at juice stalls but ask if they use filtered water to be safe.

items.label: Ais Campur (Mixed Ice)
items.text: A dessert-drink combining shaved ice, condensed milk, brown sugar syrup, and tropical fruits or beans. It's sweet and cooling—popular as an afternoon treat in hot weather.

items.label: Beer and Alcohol Availability
items.text: Alcohol is widely available in urban areas and tourist regions but less so in conservative Muslim neighborhoods. Bintang and Anker are the main local beers; spirits are expensive due to taxes.

items.label: Tap Water and Ice Safety
items.text: Tap water is not safe to drink; order bottled water (air mineral) or drinks made with boiled water. Ice at tourist restaurants is typically fine; street stall ice can be risky.

items.label: Tuak (Traditional Rice Wine)
items.text: A fermented alcoholic drink from palm sap or rice, particularly popular in Sumatra and parts of Bali. It's low alcohol, sweet, and often homemade—sample it at local celebrations or dedicated shops, but quality and hygiene vary.

items.label: Right Hand for Eating and Passing
items.text: In Indonesian culture, the left hand is traditionally considered unclean. Use your right hand to eat, pass dishes, and hand over money, even if you're left-handed—it's a sign of respect.

items.label: Communal Dining
items.text: Indonesian meals are often shared family-style with multiple dishes in the center. Take small portions and refill your plate rather than heaping everything on at once.

items.label: Tipping is Not Obligatory
items.text: Tipping is not customary in warungs or street stalls. Upscale restaurants may include a 10% service charge; if not, a small tip (5–10%) is appreciated but never expected.

items.label: Remove Shoes When Entering Homes
items.text: If invited to dine at someone's home, remove your shoes at the entrance. Wait to be shown where to sit and don't start eating until the host begins.

items.label: Compliment the Food
items.text: Indonesians take pride in their cooking. Saying 'Enak!' (Delicious!) or 'Lezat!' (Tasty!) is always appreciated and costs nothing.

items.label: Finishing Your Plate
items.text: Leaving food on your plate can be seen as wasteful or disrespectful, especially in poorer areas. Take only what you can eat; it's acceptable to ask for a smaller portion.
